core_intuitions: The gradient is perpendicular to the loss contours and points uphill; -gradient is the steepest-descent direction, and the directional derivative along any other direction is just the projection ||grad|| cos(phi) onto it.
Curvature (the Hessian's eigenvalues) tells you the shape of the local landscape: all-positive = bowl/minimum, mixed sign = saddle, near-zero = flat plateau.

The condition number kappa = L/mu is the ratio of steepest-to-shallowest curvature; large kappa makes plain GD zigzag across the steep axis and crawl along the shallow one. Momentum damps the zigzag (~sqrt(kappa) steps) and Adam preconditions each axis so the elongated bowl looks nearly circular (near-straight path).
Neural loss surfaces are non-convex: permutation symmetry creates many equivalent valleys, and in high dimension almost every critical point is a saddle, not a local minimum.

Flat minima generalize better than sharp minima: a small weight perturbation (which stands in for distribution shift) barely changes the loss in a wide basin but blows it up in a narrow spike; SGD's gradient noise biases the search toward the flat basins.

new_figures.name: gradient-contour-steepest-descent
new_figures.concept: Gradient geometry on a loss contour map: grad points uphill perpendicular to contours, -grad descends fastest, and the directional derivative is the cosine projection.
new_figures.rationale: This is the chapter's opening claim and the single geometric fact all of gradient descent rests on. Prose states 'grad points in the direction of steepest increase' and gives D_u f = ||grad|| cos(phi), but the reader only truly grasps it when they SEE the arrow standing perpendicular to the level sets and the projection onto an off-axis direction. Nothing else in the chapter conveys this.
new_figures.placement: The Gradient: Steepest Ascent in Parameter Space
new_figures.brief: A 2D parameter plane, theta_1 horizontal and theta_2 vertical, filled with a few nested smooth elliptical contour lines of a loss (concentric, drawn thin in v-grid), with the minimum at the center marked theta* by a small dot. Label the contours qualitatively as increasing loss outward (e.g. an outer contour tagged 'higher loss', an inner one 'lower loss') -- no numeric loss values. Pick a point P sitting on one of the mid contours. From P draw a bold arrow in v-accent pointing radially OUTWARD, exactly perpendicular to the tangent of the contour at P, labeled 'grad f  (steepest ASCENT)'. From P draw the opposite arrow pointing INWARD toward theta*, labeled '-grad f  (gradient-descent step)'. Also from P draw a second, thinner arrow u at an angle phi away from grad f (say ~50 degrees), labeled 'arbitrary direction u'; drop a dashed perpendicular from the tip of a unit-length u onto the grad-f line to show the projection, annotated 'D_u f = ||grad f|| cos(phi)'. Add a small boxed callout near P: 'gradient is perpendicular to the contour lines'. A short caption strip: 'descent is fastest when you move straight down the gradient (phi = 180 degrees)'. Keep all four labels non-overlapping with clear leader lines. Theme-safe classes only (v-grid contours, v-accent arrows, v-stroke axes, v-muted secondary text); works in light and dark. Everything inside the viewBox.

new_figures.name: optimizer-paths-conditioning
new_figures.concept: GD vs Momentum vs Adam trajectories on an ill-conditioned (elongated) quadratic bowl, showing zigzag, damped oscillation, and preconditioned near-straight descent.
new_figures.rationale: This one figure unifies the largest slice of the chapter: the GD/Momentum/Adam variants table, the condition-number section (kappa = L/mu, the sqrt(kappa) momentum speedup), the worked 2D quadratic example, and the runnable code experiment (which literally computes these three trajectories). The whole point -- 'why does momentum accelerate and why does Adam not care about conditioning' -- only becomes intuitive when you watch the three paths on the same elongated contours. This is the payoff figure of the chapter.
new_figures.brief: Main panel: an anisotropic bowl drawn as a set of nested ELONGATED elliptical contours -- stretched wide along the horizontal axis and narrow (high curvature) along the vertical axis -- representing the chapter's own example L = theta_1^2 + 10*theta_2^2 (condition number kappa = 10). Mark the minimum at the center as theta*. From a single common start point near the upper edge (off to one side so the long axis is traversed), draw three trajectories as polylines of discrete steps ending at theta*: (1) GD in a neutral v-muted color -- a pronounced ZIGZAG that bounces back and forth across the narrow vertical axis while inching slowly along the horizontal axis; label 'GD: overshoots the steep axis, crawls the shallow axis (~kappa steps)'. (2) Momentum in a secondary accent -- a damped, smoother oscillation that overshoots once or twice then settles, clearly fewer bounces; label 'Momentum: velocity cancels the zigzag (~sqrt(kappa) steps)'. (3) Adam in v-accent -- a nearly STRAIGHT diagonal path to the minimum; label 'Adam: rescales each axis -> near-isotropic -> near-straight'. Add a small inset (bottom corner) with two little shapes side by side: an elongated ellipse labeled 'kappa = L/mu large' and an arrow to a near-circle labeled 'after preconditioning: kappa ~ 1', captioning why Adam's path is straight. Include a compact three-row legend keyed by color. Annotate a small note: 'step counts are illustrative; kappa here = 10 = ratio of the two curvatures'. No invented benchmark numbers. Theme-safe classes, legible in both themes, all trajectories and labels non-overlapping and inside the viewBox.

new_figures.name: loss-landscape-convex-vs-nonconvex
new_figures.concept: Side-by-side convex bowl vs non-convex neural terrain, marking global min, local min, a saddle with up/down curvature, and a flat plateau.
new_figures.rationale: The chapter currently conveys this with a crude ASCII sketch (lines 133-143). The convexity section, the 'why neural nets are non-convex' list, and the saddle-points section all describe a landscape the reader cannot picture. A clean two-panel infographic replaces the ASCII and makes concrete the three structural facts the chapter emphasizes: one basin vs many, saddles curving up-and-down, and flat plateaus where grad ~ 0 but you are far from a minimum.
new_figures.brief: Two panels side by side, both with a vertical 'Loss' axis and horizontal 'theta (parameter)' axis (single-parameter cross-section framing). LEFT panel titled 'Convex': one smooth U-shaped bowl, a single dot at the bottom labeled 'global min theta*', and a short note 'every local min IS the global min; GD with eta <= 1/L is guaranteed to converge'. RIGHT panel titled 'Non-convex (neural networks)': a wavy terrain curve with several features clearly marked -- a shallow basin dot labeled 'local min', a deeper basin dot labeled 'global min', and on a ridge between two basins a dot labeled 'saddle' with two tiny curvature arrows at it (one small arc curving UP across the ridge, one curving DOWN along it) and the note 'grad L = 0, curves up one way, down another'. Include one broad, gently-sloping SHADED stretch labeled 'plateau: grad L ~ 0 (dead ReLU / saturation)'. Below the right panel a one-line cause strip: 'permutation symmetry (many equal valleys)  .  saddles dominate in high dimension  .  flat plateaus'. Use v-accent for the marked min/saddle dots, v-grid for axes and gridlines, v-muted for secondary text, v-fill for the shaded plateau region. Keep it uncluttered; this must read cleanly as a static frame in both light and dark themes, everything within the viewBox.

new_figures.name: flat-vs-sharp-minima-generalization
new_figures.concept: A wide flat minimum vs a narrow sharp minimum, with a shifted test-loss curve showing flatness stays low under perturbation while sharpness blows up.
new_figures.rationale: The 'why SGD generalizes' interview question is a signature payoff of this chapter, and its core mechanism -- flat basins are robust to weight perturbation (a proxy for distribution shift) while sharp spikes are not -- is inherently spatial. A static picture of a wide basin and a narrow spike with a slightly shifted overlaid curve makes the robustness argument click in one glance, far better than the prose in the Flat Regions and Interview Corner sections.
new_figures.brief: A single 'Loss' vs 'theta' plot containing two basins along the horizontal axis: on the left a WIDE, gently-walled basin (the flat minimum) and on the right a NARROW, steep V/spike (the sharp minimum). Both bottoms reach roughly the SAME low training-loss level -- draw a faint horizontal guide so the reader sees they are equally good on TRAIN. Draw the train-loss curve as a solid line (v-stroke). Overlay a second, slightly horizontally-SHIFTED copy of the same landscape as a dashed line in v-accent, labeled 'test loss (distribution shift ~ small weight perturbation Delta theta)'. At the flat minimum place a ball marker and annotate 'flat: small Delta theta -> small Delta loss -> generalizes' (show that the dashed curve directly above the ball is still low). At the sharp minimum place a ball marker and annotate 'sharp: same Delta theta -> large Delta loss -> generalizes worse' (show the dashed curve directly above that ball shooting high, with a small vertical gap bracket illustrating the loss jump). Near the flat basin ball add a small jitter cloud of a few dots labeled 'SGD gradient noise -> can't sit in a narrow spike, biased toward flat basins'. Keep annotations short, use leader lines, no overlaps. Solid = train (v-stroke), dashed = shifted test (v-accent), balls in v-accent. Theme-safe, legible in both themes, all content inside the viewBox.

summary: This concept-rich chapter has ZERO figures, so the plan proposes four foundational static infographics and no keep/refine of existing art. (1) gradient-contour-steepest-descent grounds the chapter's opening geometric claim -- gradient perpendicular to contours, -gradient descends fastest, directional derivative as cosine projection. (2) optimizer-paths-conditioning is the unifying payoff figure: GD zigzag vs Momentum damping vs Adam's preconditioned near-straight path on an elongated (kappa=10) bowl, tying together the variants table, the condition-number theory, the worked quadratic, and the runnable code. (3) loss-landscape-convex-vs-nonconvex replaces the weak ASCII sketch with a clean two-panel convex-bowl-vs-neural-terrain diagram marking global/local minima, a saddle with up/down curvature, and a flat plateau. (4) flat-vs-sharp-minima-generalization (priority 2, the trimmable one) makes the 'why SGD generalizes' interview argument visual via a wide basin vs a narrow spike under a shifted test-loss curve. A backprop/chain-rule figure is deliberately NOT proposed here because the chapter explicitly defers that derivation to the dedicated autodiff and neural-nets-from-scratch chapters. All figures are theme-safe, use only the chapter's own illustrative integers (kappa=10 from L = theta_1^2 + 10 theta_2^2), invent no benchmark numbers, and read fully as static frames.
